name: Lahar
description: Lahar, in Sumerian mythology, is the god of cattle. He is considered one of the essential deities responsible for the prosperity and sustenance of humankind. Lahar was created by the chief god Enlil, along with his sister Ashnan, the goddess of grain, to provide food and resources for the Anunnaki, the major gods. Their creation was crucial because the Anunnaki were struggling to sustain themselves. Lahar and Ashnan represented the bounty of the earth, Lahar specifically embodying the vital role of livestock in providing meat, milk, and other resources. He is seen as a benevolent deity, contributing directly to the well-being of both gods and humans. The myth concerning Lahar and Ashnan emphasizes the importance of agriculture and animal husbandry in Sumerian society. Although often overshadowed by more prominent deities, Lahar's role was fundamental to the Sumerian worldview, reflecting their deep connection to and reliance on the land and its resources. He represents the domesticated animals, particularly cattle, that were integral to their survival and economy. Without Lahar and Ashnan, the Sumerians believed that neither the gods nor humanity could flourish.
subtitle: God of Cattle, Sustainer of Life

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Lahar and Ashnan
keyMyths.description: Enlil created Lahar and Ashnan to provide sustenance for the Anunnaki, who were struggling to feed themselves. Their existence was crucial for the well-being of both gods and humans.

keyMyths.title: Lahar and Ashnan: A Debate
keyMyths.description: A myth describes a debate between Lahar and Ashnan about which contributes more to society. This myth highlights the importance of both livestock and agriculture.

keyMyths.title: Lahar's Blessing
keyMyths.description: Lahar's blessing ensures the health and fertility of cattle, providing a stable source of food and resources for the Sumerians. His favor was sought for successful animal husbandry.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
